RoLLESTON, M.A M1OF COm0 to deial 'Wih a feature of Davis' s influence and work #hich  gives him a unique   position in the history of Irish patriotism And first 1 must ask you to cast a, glance hack upon 1 much earlier period of Irish lhistory _a period, in fact, when modern Irish  history be  saic to have begun, after the Battle of the Bovne. It FaS nay then that the modern Irish nation, 45 we know it now , hegan to be shaped, and it was then that tIo great streams of  patriotic thought and efiort took their rise, and dominated the whole of Irish history from that day to this_ And it is a Olrious fact that these two historic of Irish thought originated with two dignitaries of the Pro- streans testant Church in Ireland. I need hardly name them to you--ou will think at once of Dean Swift and Bishop Berkeley. Swift, on   the one in modern Ireland, the principle of defence against hand , represented, enemies from without  the protection of Irish commerce and iudustly and political rights against the aggression by '#hich they Fere then Berkoley, on  the other hand, bade the systematically  trampled out. Irish mind turn its eyes homeward and  inward_he summoned Irish- men to hethink themselves not only of what they had to endure and to repel in the iay of foreign oppression , but the great field of reform which Ireland could cultivate for herself even under existing conditions and or leavo from any foreign   poirer: Swift hecame the without let of the line of Irish leaders running through  Maly- spiritual ancestor nouX , Wolfe-Tone, 0'Connell, the Fenian leaders, Isaac Butt, down to of tho presont day. The Iino of Parnell and tho political loaders descent from   Berkeley Ias naturally produced  namcs less conspicuous some   provalent nationl vioa OIL tho stago of history ~they adtacked they wcre numbered among tho host of quict like Father Mathew 1 Or and devoted 'workors who sct themsolvcs to make two blades of grass they emhodied thomselves in grow whero ono grew heforo ; oflen Dublin Soaiety, and in tho admirablo   ingtitutiong liko tho Royal

present day they arC brilliantly represented by Sir Horace Plunkett, and the able and devoted group of MCnL and Fomen whom he has gathered   about him, among them the poet, thinker and publicis   who i9 editor of that most entertaining and thought provoking paper, tho Irish Homestead, upon whom 1 sometimes think more of the mantle af Davis #as ifallen ihan upon any ma41 of Ou time. Now I do not know   why it should be s0, but by some fatality it has cer- tainly   happened that the children of Swift have very rarely been friendly nd  helpful to the children of   Berkeley. The two streams flowed down Inish history in distinct and  separate channels. But the Fonderful  thing   about Davis-it must be wonderful because it is 60 uncommon  is that in lim- for once the streams flowed together the spirit of  Berkeley's 0( Querist, )) that treasurehouse of   practical wisdom Fas evory whit aS strong as the spivit of the Drapier' $ 1 Letters. 1 ) He would not   merely have Irishmen made free_he   would have ithem much moro rorthy of being free. He Was forever holding up to his  countrymen "ith the persuasive earnestness, the contagious enthusiasm of uhhich he had the cecret, 1 noble ideal of Irish manhood to be attained not by armed  force, not by voting for it, not by plat- form oratory, hut by self-discipline, self-control, self-education_ He knew that in the ruined, negleoted   garden  of Irish life and character many evil things had struck root the flaunting reed and tho bitter thorn. 1] Tlhese he set himsolf to eradicate with just the same energy as that which he spent, and olled others to spend, in keeping at bay the external forces of'  destruction . His essays   teen with suggestions of practical reforms that Irishmen  could at once undertake for them- selves _his diaries and letters   show   him  continually on  the wntch for weak places in Trich life to make gtrong, for 'waste places to till and plant. Many  things  have   since Ibeen taken up and done, but there i6 cnough left to give uS  plenty to think of and work a for many a day to como. I have dlwelt on this side of Davis' $ ork because 1 think it is ofiten too much   ovor-shadowed 'by tho more romantic appcwal made by hig poli itics and Iis poebry. Wlat Duvis iglt lve done in the latter phero, if Ie had lived for  llitematuro imstead   of using literaturo for lifo, there is mo telling naw . Ho wroto verse, in tha main, not becnuso ho felt any orcativo impulse, mny  passionato inecdl to express hinself in  thal form, hub   mainly Icanuso ho  justly folt it to bo a powerful

agoncy both for  immeliato political objects, and also for that vivify- ing and ennobling of Irish history in the popular mind IF hich, as I havo sid before, Was Qu0 of Uhe m0W forccs that he brought into Irish life. Much of tho rerso, dramatic and fiery as it IWuS , n-hich he dashed off for the wcekly columns of The Nation haS, of  Courso, only tho temporary valuo of most prosa journalism . But into certain poems, such as tho Lament for Owen   Roo and tho Sack of Baltimore, Davis put his wholo strength; and these have, I think, 4 depth of passion, a rich  and  sensitive music, a strong simplicity and  sincerity which shon" that his name would   havo bcen remembcred in  literaturo eren if it had no othor claims to ou remembrance and our reverence. But his claims, in faot, were 60 many and great that I cannot pre- tend to gire an adeqauto account of them here. Threo years _mostly spent in anonymous journalism__without   realth, influenco, porerful connerions ; it 'Ias 1 short timo in which to carve for himself so noblo and enduring a record in tho heart of 2 nation . And Davis   did this, not by intellectual brillianay , not by artistiic accomplishment, least of all by successful 01' social  organization, for all he iand his friends d1d, o bogan to d0, in this Jino rs soon engulfed in tho vast dis- aster of ithe Famine The Fronch moralist, Vauvenargues, has beautiful sentenco which   has olten   recurred to me in thinking about Davis and the peculiar fragrance of his momory (( Great thoughts have their source in the heart Los grandes pensees viennent du COBUT , No man Ma $ more courageous than Davis, no man moro truth- ful, no man moro methodic and exact in all his undertakings. Duffy has told us that his friends had tho eamo oareless   confidence in him that they had in tho operations of Naturo. But courago, and veracity, and method, mav often be harsh and wounding and   repellent__To rarely find without s0mo tnaco of this element. In then, indeed, Davig they had of it ho sicctnoss and tendernoss of his mone naturg ran through ovorything ho said nnd  did. Evory thought he had for hie novcr from tho arid passion f country'8   ielfare   sprang, national   ogotism, hut from a' deop  human   sympabhy  with tha culfer- inge, of tho  pcoplo, and  al8o with tho grcat latont tho degradlations, powors, tho nollo   human   qualitiog  which h1o   longod t0 e00   lilenated and tho things  which ho  did, and developed . And so iwith all grcat which ho   inspired othcrs to achiovo, it ho planned, and which remains Ian I gnid ab first, thad the granbost logacy ho loft u8 Wa.$ truq,

the gift af himself__tho revelation of 1 human temper and character; tho finest, I think, that ever rere displayed  in the   public   affairs o Ireland . And if we lament that such & character and infuence were too goon withdranu from the service of Ireland, let uS remember that Davis was mercifully taken any from the evil to come. Neither he nor any other livng man could have   coped Tith the appalling catastropho of the Famine, which follored almost immediately on his death ; and one coul not lave desired for S0 passionate  and sengitive a nature, the experience of   watching helplesslv tho agony of his country in that most   awful hour of her  luistory. Let   his image, therefore, remain with uS eternally young and bright, with no stain of failure O1 disaster on his brow_~an image  speuking of hope,, of valour, and above a]l of unquenchable faith in high ideals, to all  generations of his country men,

Tho first completo Folumo of Kottahos, consisting of twelvo parts, wAS issucd in 1874, and similar volumes in 1877 and 1881, With the appearance o thc first Dart of vol. iv. in Hilary term, 1882, and apparontly in tho heyday of success it  suddenly ceased, to thg great regret of all  concerned. A letter from the publisher to Walter   Ham- ilton, editor of Parodies, nOW before uS, says : The first num- bor of vol. iv. is the Jast published of Kottabos. It is a great pitz it Kas allored to die out, but tho fact is Prof: Tyrrell found his time too much occupied with other matters and, a5 tho Americans 6ay, he let it slide. 9) A glanco through tho list of contributors to the first series rereals such 'Fell-known names 95 tho Editor, Dr . Salater Hamlet's Soliloquy into Hebrew, whilst for the excellence of the original Ferso tho names  mentioned abova aro a   sufficient guaranteo. 'The first  attempt to resuccitate it FaS made when Mr. Rolleston started tho Dublin   University Review 1) in 1885, and devoted 2 few pages of it under tho old title to tle old subjects_ But 4 vory free and, racy translation from  Theocritus bby Dr. Kenningalo Cook, in the Decomher part, Anttered the dovecots of Dublin, raised the ire of the unco guid, and  caused the  experiment to le  abandoned, Iu  Hilary term, 1888, Prof. J. In hic prefaco ho falls into a curioug Crror, saying tho last number appcared in 1881," whereas, 48 Wo have   shown, it really appoared tho   following ycar: 'Amongst the bbetter knowa  contrihutors Woro T. IRISH LITERARY  SOCIETY .
On 4th November tho first Tea Tablo Talk of this season Fag given by Dr. J. S. Crone, J.P., #ho had chosen for his subject the works of Edmund Downey Novelist and Humorist. Mr. A P. Graves   presided, and thero mas 1 crowded attendanco. The   speaker said that on  thein last annual   excursion, when   gratefully  discussing the Farm welcomo accorded them   by Mr . Downey in  Waterford, he found that some of the   younger members wera not 5o rell acquainted with that  gentle man's works as they should he, and ho told them so. Mr Graves then suggested  this Talk J ) as a means to their cnlightenmont, a pleasant task, which he would attempt   horever inadequately  equipped , Mr , run the whole gamut from 00 Downey , Ias a literary man, had one line jokes 2) and ocoasional verse,  through shilling shockers, tho old I three  decker )} novels, to dramas (yet  unactod), and recently bro  graphy ad serious history. It might hardly be too much to say that like hig illustrious countryman ho touched  nothing that he did not adorn . In aaddition he had in Trenty Years Ago given uS 0 pleasant book of literary anecdote ind reminiscence into which it Fas alwaye to dip. As tho so1 of i Waterford   shipbrokor, Mr. a delight Downoy in his had ovidently  mixod  much iith the old salts, youth Anchor Watch Yarns, 1} Bailormen and crimps,  whom To meet in V( )) other ealy novels . Theso havo tho Capt. Lanagan' 8 Log; and and gainod for bheir creator tho title of truo    smack of to briney, Whcn about two and twenty tho Marryatt of tho moroantile marino. Mr . Downey camo to London, andl through tho influenco of his cousin, Richard TTingloy' g  Mngazino, ho obtained 4 Dowling, then ` oditing well  known publishing firm_ Hero ho   turned position in that onco tho cditorial  chair, and his scribbler  himgell, gucceeded   Dowling   in sketches e improssed  William  Tiusloy, # shrowd judge, that ho oarly

insisted on publishing his first work in two volumes, to tho astonish- ment and delight of the tyro. After  four" pleasant years Mr. Downey started A8 a pul blisher, but still, under the pen-name of F. M. Allen; wrote many 1 side-splitting sketch, pseudo-historical romance, and seneational novel. Digressing for a moment, the speaker said it should not pass unnotod in that Socicty that 18 a publisher Mr Downey fostered Irish letterg in this metropolis when every other publisher looked askance. He  could mention one or two names famous to-day who owed not a littlo to his  enterprise, and he believed that somo of his ventures, such aS Hibernia Pacata,' J) The Irish Novelists' Library, aud an edition of Lefanu #erc undertaken less a5 monef- making   speculations than   fron 1 pride   in the authors of his natire Jand, As a serious novelist  MT. Dorney is seen at his best in The Merchant of Killogue 0) and Clashmore, nhich reveal an intimate knowledga of southern middle-class Irish life of peasant and shop keeper. As a, humorist he struck 1 nCFF vein, and in his historical incongruities he has heen compared to 0( Mark  Twain with the mellow Munster brogue. 1) In other quarters he Fas hailed as 2 second Lever; and it is said that Mr. Gladstone, in his old age, found solace and re ceation in reading the nacy historical disquisitions of Dan Banim , that Milesian Homer to whom 'e Oiw @ ( "The Voyage of rthe Ark, Through Green Glasses, and From  the Green Bag He would read 1' few selections, though he felt it was liko advertising houses by showing a brick or two. They should relad tlhe books for themselves. Dr . Crone then read  selections   fiom Ballybeg Junction, Brayhard, Dun- leary, and others, to tho evident enjoyment of the audience. Mr . Graves   said ho could endorso all that had been said by Dr, Crone regarding Mr. Downey a8 author and publisher, a9 he had edited Lefanu' g poems in the series mentioned. It should n0t be forgotten that Mr. Downey W4s also ono of the founders of that Society , and tho committco had met iin his   house before thez had 1 homo of their Own . Mr . J, W, Joynt moved and Miss 0'Flynu seconded the vole of thanks. pleasant reunion, ably organised  by Miss 0'Flyun, was held o1 21sit  Novcmiher ,  when ovCr a hhundrcd   guasts {btendec , The choir  of girls   from Uho Norlh London   Cbllegiate  School,  beautifully rendered somo old songs of our Iand, and Misg Clarko told  somo old folk tules admirably . preciated, and  Miss Titty MacVeagh   fairly brought down the houso by her inimitablo recitation of sclections  from Moira 0' Ncill," and Mrs. Twiggloty J) aS in  Cathal 0'Byrae's delightful sketches of that northern worthy.
EDITOR'S GOSSTP .
The   latest newcomer in tho ranks of Dublin   journalism is (( The National   Volunteer; the   wcekly  oflicial   organ of the National Volun- teers, It is  ably edlited bby Mr. John P .
